title_user: My breasts are rock hard and painful
peak_timing: Most common around day 3–5 after birth when mature milk comes in.
what_it_is: Engorgement happens when your breasts suddenly fill with more milk and fluid than your baby is removing. The breasts can feel hard, hot, and very full. It is uncomfortable but usually temporary as feeding patterns settle.
body_signals: Breasts feel very full, firm, or rock hard
Skin looks shiny or stretched
Mild warmth or aching in the breasts
baby_signals: Baby struggles to latch onto very firm breast
Baby may slip off the nipple frequently
Baby appears frustrated at the breast

immediate_relief_steps.title: Soften the areola before feeds
immediate_relief_steps.description: Apply a warm compress for a few minutes, then gently hand-express a small amount of milk until the area around the nipple feels softer so baby can latch more easily.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Feed frequently on demand
immediate_relief_steps.description: Offer the breast often, at least every 2–3 hours, to help remove milk and relieve fullness.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Use cold packs after feeds
immediate_relief_steps.description: Apply a cold pack or chilled cabbage leaves over a thin cloth for 10–15 minutes after feeds to reduce swelling and discomfort.

dos: Do feed your baby often, even if feeds are shorter
Do use gentle hand expression if baby cannot latch
Do wear a supportive but not tight bra
donts: Do not suddenly stop breastfeeding
Do not bind your breasts tightly
Do not apply strong, direct pressure to painful areas
when_to_expect_improvement: With frequent effective feeds and comfort measures, engorgement usually improves noticeably within 24–48 hours.
red_flags: Fever above 38 °C
One area of the breast becomes very red, hot, and tender
Flu-like body aches and chills
recommended_action_if_red_flags: If you develop fever or a hot, red, very painful area on the breast, contact your doctor or local emergency service as soon as possible.
disclaimer: This information is for general education only and is not a substitute for professional medical care. If you are worried, seek help from a qualified health professional.

title_user: My nipples are cracked and very sore
peak_timing: Often appears in the first 1–2 weeks when latch and positioning are still being learned.
what_it_is: Cracked nipples usually mean the nipple is rubbing or being pinched during feeds, most often from a shallow latch or awkward position. They can be very sore but almost always improve once the latch is corrected.
body_signals: Visible cracks or scabs on the nipple surface
Burning or stinging pain during and after feeds
Occasional small spots of blood on breast pads
baby_signals: Baby latched on only to the nipple tip instead of a deep mouthful of breast
Clicking sounds during feeding
Baby slipping on and off the breast

immediate_relief_steps.title: Break latch and reattach deeply
immediate_relief_steps.description: If a feed is painful, gently insert a clean finger into the corner of baby's mouth to break the suction and try again with a deeper latch.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Use a comfortable position
immediate_relief_steps.description: Experiment with positions like laid-back or side-lying that reduce pressure on damaged areas while keeping baby close and supported.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Support healing between feeds
immediate_relief_steps.description: Express a few drops of breastmilk and dab onto the nipple, then let it air-dry. Use a breathable breast pad and avoid harsh soaps.

dos: Do seek latch help from a lactation professional if pain continues
Do start feeds on the less sore side if possible
Do keep nipples dry between feeds
donts: Do not pull baby off the breast without breaking suction
Do not scrub nipples with cloths or soap
Do not ignore persistent pain that makes you dread feeds
when_to_expect_improvement: With a better latch and gentle care, many mothers notice less pain within a few days and visible healing within 1–2 weeks.
red_flags: Sharp, burning nipple pain that worsens after weeks of painless feeds
Shiny or flaky skin on the nipple and areola
Deep breast pain that continues between feeds
recommended_action_if_red_flags: If pain is severe, getting worse, or not improving with latch changes, contact a lactation consultant or doctor to rule out infection or other causes.
disclaimer: This information is for general education only and is not a substitute for professional medical care.
source_notes: Cracked nipples linked to latch issues and possible thrush.

title_user: Breastfeeding hurts from the first latch
peak_timing: Common in the first days and weeks as you and baby are learning technique.
what_it_is: A shallow latch means baby has mostly the nipple in their mouth instead of a deep mouthful of breast. This can cause significant pain and nipple damage, and may also reduce milk transfer.
body_signals: Intense toe-curling pain at the start of each feed
Nipple comes out flattened, creased, or lipstick-shaped
Nipple skin becomes red or damaged
baby_signals: Baby's lips are tucked in instead of flanged out
More of the nipple than the areola is visible during feeds
Frequent clicking or slipping off the breast

immediate_relief_steps.title: Start with a wide-open mouth
immediate_relief_steps.description: Tickle baby's upper lip with your nipple and wait for a very wide yawn-like opening before bringing baby quickly and closely to the breast.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Check positioning and support
immediate_relief_steps.description: Keep baby's head, neck, and body in a straight line, tummy facing you, and bring baby to the breast rather than leaning forward.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Relatch when pain stays high
immediate_relief_steps.description: If the pain does not ease after the first few sucks, gently stop the feed and try again with a deeper latch instead of pushing through intense pain.

dos: Do watch for flanged lips and rounded cheeks during feeds
Do seek hands-on help if you are unsure about latch
Do use laid-back or side-lying positions if they are more comfortable
donts: Do not ignore severe pain that makes you tense or tearful
Do not push on the back of baby's head, which can cause them to pull away
Do not assume pain is 'normal' and must be endured
when_to_expect_improvement: With practice and small adjustments, many mothers notice less pain over several feeds and clear improvement within a few days.
red_flags: Nipple wounds that are worsening rather than healing
Baby seems unsatisfied after long feeds and makes few wet diapers
Baby's weight gain is poor or dropping
recommended_action_if_red_flags: If latch pain continues or baby's output or weight worries you, contact a lactation consultant or your baby's doctor promptly.
disclaimer: This is general information only and does not replace individual medical advice.
source_notes: Focus on latch correction and early pain.

title_user: I have a red, painful breast and feel like I have the flu
peak_timing: Can occur any time milk is not draining well, often in the first weeks.
what_it_is: Mastitis is inflammation of the breast, sometimes with infection. It often starts with a blocked area that is not draining well and can progress quickly. Early attention can prevent worsening.
body_signals: A wedge-shaped red or hot area on one breast
Fever, chills, or flu-like body aches
Severe tenderness in a specific area of the breast
baby_signals: Baby may fuss or pull off on the affected side if flow feels different
Baby otherwise may feed normally

immediate_relief_steps.title: Continue feeding or expressing
immediate_relief_steps.description: Keep milk moving from the affected breast with frequent feeds or gentle pumping, starting on the sore side if you can tolerate it.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Use comfort measures
immediate_relief_steps.description: Apply warm compresses before feeds and cool packs after feeds. Rest as much as possible and drink fluids.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Monitor symptoms closely
immediate_relief_steps.description: Note the size of the red area, your temperature, and how you feel overall over the next few hours.

dos: Do seek medical review urgently if you have fever or strong flu-like symptoms
Do continue breastfeeding unless your doctor advises otherwise
Do rest and ask for help with household tasks
donts: Do not stop breastfeeding abruptly
Do not ignore worsening pain, redness, or fever
Do not massage aggressively, which can increase inflammation
when_to_expect_improvement: With prompt treatment, many mothers feel noticeably better within 24–48 hours. If symptoms are not improving, medical review is essential.
red_flags: Fever above 38 °C lasting more than 24 hours
Expanding red, hot, or swollen area on the breast
Feeling very unwell, dizzy, or faint
recommended_action_if_red_flags: Contact your doctor or emergency services immediately if you have ongoing fever, spreading redness, or feel very unwell. You may need antibiotics or further assessment.
disclaimer: This information is not a diagnosis and does not replace urgent medical review if you are very unwell.

title_user: I'm worried my milk supply is too low
peak_timing: Common in the first weeks as feeding patterns and expectations are still settling.
what_it_is: Many mothers worry about milk supply, especially when baby wants to feed often. True low supply is less common than it feels and is best judged by baby's diapers, weight gain, and feeding effectiveness.
body_signals: Breasts may feel softer and less full after the early days
Letdown sensation may be mild or not felt
Pumping output may be lower than expected
baby_signals: Baby wants to feed frequently, especially in the evenings
Baby has periods of fussiness or cluster feeding
Wet and dirty diapers may be normal or reduced

immediate_relief_steps.title: Check diaper counts and weight patterns
immediate_relief_steps.description: Track the number of wet and dirty diapers in 24 hours and review your baby's recent weight checks to get an objective picture.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Feed more often and switch sides
immediate_relief_steps.description: Offer both breasts at each feed and feed on demand, including at night, to signal your body to make more milk.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Protect your rest and nutrition
immediate_relief_steps.description: Eat regular meals, drink to thirst, and rest whenever possible, as exhaustion and stress can make feeding feel harder.

dos: Do speak with your baby's doctor or a lactation consultant about your concerns
Do allow frequent feeding, including cluster feeds
Do consider a weighted feed or professional assessment if available
donts: Do not rely on pump output alone to judge your supply
Do not introduce formula or bottles suddenly without a plan if breastfeeding is your goal
Do not ignore signs of poor weight gain or very few wet diapers
when_to_expect_improvement: With frequent effective feeds and support, supply usually adjusts over several days. Emotional reassurance can also make feeds feel more manageable.
red_flags: Fewer than the expected number of wet diapers for baby's age
Baby seems very sleepy and difficult to wake for feeds
Baby's weight gain is slow or dropping
recommended_action_if_red_flags: If diapers are very few or weight gain worries you, contact your baby's doctor urgently for assessment and a feeding plan.
disclaimer: This is general educational information and does not replace personalised medical or lactation advice.
source_notes: Normalises concern while flagging true low supply signs.

title_user: My baby wants to feed again and again all evening
peak_timing: Common in the first weeks, especially in the evenings and during growth spurts.
what_it_is: Cluster feeding is when baby wants many short feeds close together, often in the late afternoon or evening. It is usually normal and helps boost your milk supply during growth spurts.
body_signals: Breasts may feel repeatedly used and softer by the end of the evening
You may feel tired and touched-out from frequent feeds
baby_signals: Baby roots and cues to feed soon after finishing a feed
Baby may be fussy or unsettled between these close-together feeds
Baby eventually has a longer stretch of sleep

immediate_relief_steps.title: Expect and plan for the pattern
immediate_relief_steps.description: Know that cluster feeding in the evening is common and often temporary. Arrange simple meals and extra help at this time if possible.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Use comfortable positions
immediate_relief_steps.description: Feed in a reclined or side-lying position so you can rest your body while baby feeds frequently.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Share the load emotionally
immediate_relief_steps.description: Ask your partner or support person to bring you water, food, or take over burping and diaper changes between feeds.

dos: Do respond to baby's hunger cues during cluster periods
Do remind yourself this pattern is usually normal and self-limited
Do rest more at other times of the day if evenings are intense
donts: Do not assume cluster feeding always means low milk supply
Do not ignore your own need for food and hydration
Do not hesitate to ask for household help
when_to_expect_improvement: Cluster feeding often settles within a few days to weeks, especially once growth spurts pass and baby's tummy size increases.
red_flags: Baby is constantly crying and inconsolable, not just seeking the breast
Very few wet diapers despite frequent feeds
Noisy breathing, difficulty feeding, or signs of illness
recommended_action_if_red_flags: If cluster feeding is accompanied by signs of illness or poor output, contact your baby's doctor for review.
disclaimer: This card offers general reassurance and is not a substitute for medical assessment when you are worried.

title_user: My baby falls asleep at the breast after a few minutes
peak_timing: Common in the first days, especially if baby is small, jaundiced, or recovering from birth.
what_it_is: Some babies become sleepy quickly at the breast and may not take full feeds. If baby is otherwise healthy and gaining weight, this can be normal. If weight gain or diaper counts are low, it may be a concern.
body_signals: Breasts may still feel quite full after short feeds
You may not feel a strong letdown if milk is not being removed well
baby_signals: Baby falls asleep within minutes of starting a feed
Baby may suck weakly with long pauses
Baby may be difficult to wake for feeds

immediate_relief_steps.title: Use gentle wake-up techniques
immediate_relief_steps.description: Undress baby to a diaper, change the diaper, or rub baby's back and feet to help them wake and stay more alert during feeds.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Switch sides when sucking slows
immediate_relief_steps.description: When baby starts dozing and sucking slows, compress the breast gently or switch to the other side to encourage more active drinking.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Track diapers and feeding frequency
immediate_relief_steps.description: Record how often baby feeds and how many wet and dirty diapers they have each day to share with your healthcare provider.

dos: Do wake baby to feed at least as often as your healthcare provider recommends
Do seek lactation help if baby stays very sleepy at the breast
Do ask about jaundice or other medical causes if you are concerned
donts: Do not let long stretches of more than the recommended hours go by without a feed in the early days
Do not assume sleepiness is always harmless
Do not try strict schedules without input if weight gain is a concern
when_to_expect_improvement: As baby grows stronger and any early issues settle, feeds usually become longer and more alert over the first weeks.
red_flags: Very few wet or dirty diapers for age
Baby too weak or sleepy to latch or stay on the breast
Yellowing skin or eyes spreading or deepening
recommended_action_if_red_flags: Contact your baby's doctor urgently if baby is very sleepy, difficult to wake, or has low output or worsening jaundice.
disclaimer: This information is general and not a substitute for prompt evaluation of a very sleepy newborn.
source_notes: Focus on sleepy newborn at breast.

title_user: I'm worried my baby isn't gaining enough weight
peak_timing: May be noticed at early check-ups in the first weeks.
what_it_is: Slow weight gain means baby is not gaining as quickly as expected. It can have many causes including latch issues, low intake, or medical conditions and always deserves prompt attention.
body_signals: Breasts may still feel full after feeds if milk is not well removed
You may not notice many strong letdowns
baby_signals: Few wet or dirty diapers for age
Sleepy, weak, or very long or very short feeds
Persistent fussiness or signs of hunger

immediate_relief_steps.title: Arrange urgent weight check
immediate_relief_steps.description: Contact your baby's doctor or clinic to arrange a weight check and feeding assessment as soon as possible.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Increase supervised feeding opportunities
immediate_relief_steps.description: Offer the breast more often, including at night, and ensure baby is effectively swallowing and not just comfort sucking.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Record diapers and feeding details
immediate_relief_steps.description: Keep a simple log of feeds, diapers, and any expressed milk or supplements to share with your healthcare provider.

dos: Do seek professional help immediately if weight gain is poor
Do follow any feeding plan you are given, including supplements if needed
Do ask for lactation support to optimise breastfeeding
donts: Do not wait weeks hoping weight gain will catch up on its own
Do not stop recommended follow-up appointments
Do not feel you have failed; many factors affect weight gain
when_to_expect_improvement: With a clear plan and support, many babies show improved weight within days to weeks, but this should be guided and monitored by professionals.
red_flags: Baby losing weight rather than gaining after the first days
Very few wet or dirty diapers
Baby is very sleepy, floppy, or difficult to wake
recommended_action_if_red_flags: Seek urgent medical care if your baby is losing weight, has very low output, or seems very unwell.
disclaimer: This card is not a diagnosis and cannot replace urgent medical care when weight gain is a concern.

title_user: My milk sprays and baby coughs or chokes at the start of feeds
peak_timing: Often noticed after supply is established and letdowns are strong.
what_it_is: An overactive letdown is when milk flows very quickly at the start of a feed. Baby may gulp, cough, or pull off, and may be gassy later. It is usually manageable with positioning and timing.
body_signals: Strong tingling letdown sensation
Milk spraying from the nipple if baby comes off suddenly
baby_signals: Coughing, choking, or gulping at the start of feeds
Pulling off and crying shortly after latch
Gassy or fussy after feeds

immediate_relief_steps.title: Use laid-back positioning
immediate_relief_steps.description: Feed in a reclined position with baby on top so gravity slows the flow and baby can manage better.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Let the first spray go into a cloth
immediate_relief_steps.description: When you feel letdown, briefly break the latch and let the initial spray go into a cloth or container, then re-latch baby.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Burp baby often
immediate_relief_steps.description: Pause to burp baby if they seem uncomfortable or are gulping lots of air.

dos: Do keep feeds calm and unhurried
Do try positions where baby's head is higher than your nipple
Do monitor baby's weight and comfort
donts: Do not press hard on your breast to block flow
Do not pump excessively between feeds, which can increase supply further
Do not ignore persistent choking or breathing difficulty
when_to_expect_improvement: As baby grows and your supply and letdown adjust, overactive letdown often becomes less intense over weeks to months.
red_flags: Baby appears to struggle to breathe during feeds
Poor weight gain despite frequent feeds
Blue lips or pauses in breathing
recommended_action_if_red_flags: If baby has breathing difficulties or poor weight gain, seek medical assessment immediately.
disclaimer: This is general information and does not replace medical care if baby has breathing or feeding problems.
source_notes: Focus on fast flow and positioning.

title_user: I have a tender lump in my breast
peak_timing: Can occur any time milk is not draining well from a small area.
what_it_is: A blocked duct is a small area in the breast where milk flow is slowed or obstructed. It can feel like a tender lump and usually improves with frequent, effective milk removal.
body_signals: Small tender lump or firm area in one breast
Pain may increase before feeds and ease afterward
No or minimal fever
baby_signals: Baby may fuss if flow is slower on that side
Baby otherwise may feed normally

immediate_relief_steps.title: Feed frequently on the affected side
immediate_relief_steps.description: Offer the affected breast first so baby's strongest sucking helps clear the blockage.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Gentle warmth and massage
immediate_relief_steps.description: Apply warm compresses before feeds and use gentle sweeping strokes toward the nipple while feeding or expressing.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Check bra and pressure points
immediate_relief_steps.description: Avoid tight bras, underwires, or pressure from straps, bags, or fingers over the sore area.

dos: Do rest and drink to thirst to support recovery
Do vary feeding positions so baby's chin points toward the blocked area
Do monitor for any signs of infection or fever
donts: Do not massage aggressively, which can cause more inflammation
Do not ignore increasing redness or flu-like symptoms
Do not suddenly stop breastfeeding
when_to_expect_improvement: Many blocked ducts improve significantly within 24–48 hours with frequent effective drainage and comfort measures.
red_flags: Developing fever or feeling flu-like
Red, hot patch over the lump
Worsening pain instead of improvement
recommended_action_if_red_flags: If a blocked duct does not improve or you develop fever or a red hot area, contact your doctor promptly to rule out mastitis.
disclaimer: This information is not a diagnosis and does not replace medical advice.

title_user: There is a small white spot on my nipple that hurts when feeding
peak_timing: Can appear any time a tiny milk duct opening is blocked.
what_it_is: A nipple bleb is a tiny blocked milk pore that appears as a white, yellow, or clear dot on the nipple. It can cause sharp pain during feeds.
body_signals: Small white or yellow dot on the nipple surface
Sharp, pinpoint pain during letdown or sucking
baby_signals: Baby may pull off due to your reaction to pain
Feeding pattern otherwise may be normal

immediate_relief_steps.title: Use warm compresses
immediate_relief_steps.description: Apply a warm, damp cloth to the nipple before feeds to help soften the area.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Breastfeed or express frequently
immediate_relief_steps.description: Keep milk flowing by breastfeeding or expressing often, especially on the affected side.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Avoid picking at the spot
immediate_relief_steps.description: Do not try to pop or cut the bleb yourself; this can cause injury or infection.

dos: Do discuss persistent blebs with a lactation consultant or doctor
Do use comfortable positions that minimise friction at the nipple
Do keep nipples dry and avoid irritants
donts: Do not pick, squeeze, or lance the bleb at home
Do not use strong soaps or alcohol on nipples
Do not ignore worsening pain or signs of infection
when_to_expect_improvement: Some blebs resolve within a few days with warm compresses and good milk flow. Stubborn spots may need professional attention.
red_flags: Spreading redness or swelling in the nipple or areola
Fever or flu-like feeling
Increasing pain with no improvement
recommended_action_if_red_flags: If pain, redness, or swelling worsens or you feel unwell, contact your healthcare provider.
disclaimer: General educational information only; not a substitute for medical advice.

title_user: My nipples look flat or turn inward and baby can't latch
peak_timing: Often noticed in the early days when baby first attempts to latch.
what_it_is: Some nipples lie flat or invert when stimulated. Many babies can still breastfeed with support, especially as breasts soften and baby grows.
body_signals: Nipple does not protrude easily with touch or cold
Nipple may pull inward when gently pressed around the areola
baby_signals: Baby struggles to maintain latch on the breast
Baby may slip off frequently or hold only the nipple tip

immediate_relief_steps.title: Soften and shape the breast
immediate_relief_steps.description: Use reverse pressure softening or a brief hand expression before feeds to help the nipple stand out more.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Use positions with good support
immediate_relief_steps.description: Try laid-back or side-lying positions and use your hand to support the breast in a way that helps baby get more tissue into the mouth.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Seek skilled latch help
immediate_relief_steps.description: Work with a lactation consultant who can show you techniques or tools, such as temporary use of a nipple shield if appropriate.

dos: Do be patient as baby learns and your breast shape changes with feeding
Do ask for hands-on support early
Do monitor baby's weight and diaper counts
donts: Do not blame yourself; nipple shapes vary naturally
Do not use devices or suction tools without professional guidance
Do not ignore ongoing poor milk transfer
when_to_expect_improvement: As swelling settles and baby grows, many mothers find latching becomes easier over several days to weeks.
red_flags: Persistent pain and poor latch despite support
Baby not gaining weight or having few wet diapers
recommended_action_if_red_flags: If latch remains very difficult or baby's intake is low, seek urgent lactation and medical input.
disclaimer: General guidance only; not a substitute for personalised assessment.

title_user: My baby suddenly refuses to latch on one or both breasts
peak_timing: Can happen any time; often called a nursing strike when sudden.
what_it_is: Breast refusal or a nursing strike is when a baby who previously fed well begins to refuse the breast. It can be due to many reasons, from pain or illness to changes in routine.
body_signals: Fullness and discomfort if feeds are missed
Engorgement on the refused side
baby_signals: Turning away, arching back, or crying when offered the breast
Accepting bottle or expressed milk more easily
Otherwise behaving normally or sometimes irritable

immediate_relief_steps.title: Rule out obvious causes
immediate_relief_steps.description: Check for nasal congestion, ear pain, mouth sores, or changes in soaps or perfumes that might bother baby.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Offer the breast when sleepy and calm
immediate_relief_steps.description: Try feeding when baby is drowsy or just waking, in a quiet, dim environment with skin-to-skin contact.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Protect your supply
immediate_relief_steps.description: Express milk regularly if baby is not feeding at the breast to keep your supply and relieve fullness.

dos: Do stay patient and calm; forcing can make refusal worse
Do keep offering lovingly and without pressure
Do consult a lactation consultant or doctor if refusal continues
donts: Do not force baby's head onto the breast
Do not suddenly stop breastfeeding without a plan
Do not ignore signs of illness or pain in baby
when_to_expect_improvement: Many nursing strikes resolve within a few days to a week with gentle persistence and addressing underlying causes.
red_flags: Baby has signs of illness (fever, lethargy, dehydration)
Very few wet diapers while refusing the breast
Persistent refusal over several days despite support
recommended_action_if_red_flags: Seek medical and lactation support promptly if baby is refusing feeds and has any signs of dehydration or illness.
disclaimer: Information only; always seek medical care if baby is not feeding well.

title_user: Baby coughs or chokes every time my milk lets down
peak_timing: Often in the first months when supply is robust.
what_it_is: When letdown is strong, baby may briefly choke or cough as they adjust to the fast flow. This is usually manageable but should be monitored.
body_signals: Strong tingling or fullness at letdown
Milk sprays if baby pulls off during letdown
baby_signals: Coughing or pulling off at the start of the feed
Prefers one side or certain positions

immediate_relief_steps.title: Feed in an upright or laid-back position
immediate_relief_steps.description: Use gravity to slow the flow so baby has better control of swallowing.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Catch the initial spray
immediate_relief_steps.description: Let the first heavy spray go into a cloth, then bring baby back to the breast once the flow settles.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Pause if baby is overwhelmed
immediate_relief_steps.description: If baby coughs or looks distressed, gently break the latch, sit them up, and allow them to recover before offering again.

dos: Do stay attentive to baby's breathing and comfort
Do burp baby and take brief breaks if needed
Do talk to your provider if choking is frequent or severe
donts: Do not ignore any signs of breathing difficulty
Do not cover baby's nose or mouth while feeding
Do not lie baby flat on their back during feeds
when_to_expect_improvement: As baby matures and your supply balances, choking with letdown often becomes less frequent.
red_flags: Blue colour around lips or face during feeds
Pauses in breathing or very distressed coughing
Poor weight gain or frequent chest infections
recommended_action_if_red_flags: Seek urgent medical evaluation if baby shows breathing problems, colour changes, or poor weight gain.
disclaimer: This is not a substitute for prompt medical review of any breathing concerns.

title_user: Using the pump is painful on my nipples
peak_timing: Any time pumping is started or settings are changed.
what_it_is: Pumping should feel like firm pulling, not sharp pain. Painful pumping often means flange size, suction settings, or positioning need adjustment.
body_signals: Nipple rubbing hard against the tunnel of the flange
Redness, swelling, or blisters after pumping
baby_signals: Not directly affected, but may receive expressed milk

immediate_relief_steps.title: Check flange fit
immediate_relief_steps.description: Ensure your nipple moves freely in the tunnel without excessive areola being pulled in. Consider a different size if needed.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Lower suction and adjust rhythm
immediate_relief_steps.description: Use the lowest suction that still expresses milk and choose a comfortable rhythm setting.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Limit session length
immediate_relief_steps.description: Shorten sessions or take breaks if nipples feel sore, and allow time to heal if there is visible damage.

dos: Do read your pump's instructions carefully
Do try lubricant drops of clean breastmilk or pump-safe nipple cream if recommended
Do ask for help with fit from a lactation professional
donts: Do not endure severe pain during pumping
Do not use maximum suction out of the box
Do not ignore damaged skin on the nipples
when_to_expect_improvement: Once flange fit and settings are adjusted, pumping should become more comfortable over a few sessions.
red_flags: Cracked, bleeding, or blistered nipples that worsen
Signs of infection such as pus, spreading redness, or fever
recommended_action_if_red_flags: If nipple damage is significant or not healing, see a healthcare provider.
disclaimer: General guidance only; always follow the safety information for your specific pump.
source_notes: Focus on pump fit and suction.

title_user: We are using both breast and bottle and I'm feeling confused
peak_timing: Common when introducing bottles or formula alongside breastfeeding.
what_it_is: Combination feeding means using both breast and bottle (expressed milk or formula). It can work well with a plan that protects your supply and meets baby's needs.
body_signals: Breasts may feel fuller if feeds at the breast are reduced
Possible engorgement if changes are sudden
baby_signals: Preference for faster-flow bottles
Different sucking patterns at breast vs bottle

immediate_relief_steps.title: Clarify your goals
immediate_relief_steps.description: Decide what balance of breast and bottle you hope for so you and your provider can plan around it.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Match bottle feeds with expression
immediate_relief_steps.description: If baby gets a bottle of expressed milk or formula, consider expressing around that time to protect your supply.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Use paced bottle feeding
immediate_relief_steps.description: Use slow-flow teats and paced feeding so baby does not learn to prefer a very fast flow.

dos: Do discuss your combination feeding plan with a lactation consultant or doctor
Do watch for engorgement if breastfeeds drop suddenly
Do be kind to yourself; many families mix feeding methods
donts: Do not change your feeding pattern rapidly without considering your supply
Do not feel you must meet anyone else's idea of 'perfect' feeding
Do not ignore baby's hunger and fullness cues
when_to_expect_improvement: Once you have a clear, consistent routine, many feeding worries ease over days to weeks.
red_flags: Baby not gaining weight well
Severe engorgement or blocked ducts after schedule changes
Parental distress or conflict around feeding decisions
recommended_action_if_red_flags: Seek professional guidance if weight gain, your supply, or emotional strain become concerns.
disclaimer: General guidance only; your feeding plan should be personalised.

title_user: My baby prefers the bottle and refuses the breast
peak_timing: Often appears after bottles are introduced early or with fast-flow teats.
what_it_is: Some babies come to prefer the easier, faster flow of a bottle compared with the breast. This can make returning to breastfeeding harder but not always impossible.
body_signals: Engorgement or fullness if baby avoids breastfeeds
Possible drop in supply over time
baby_signals: Crying or pushing away from the breast
Eagerly taking a bottle right afterward
Frustration at slower flow at the breast

immediate_relief_steps.title: Slow bottle feeds down
immediate_relief_steps.description: Use slow-flow teats and paced bottle feeding so baby works a bit harder for milk and does not get used to a very rapid flow.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Offer breast in calm moments
immediate_relief_steps.description: Try offering the breast when baby is sleepy or just waking, without pressure, in a quiet environment.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Protect your supply
immediate_relief_steps.description: If baby is taking most feeds by bottle, consider expressing to maintain your milk supply while you work on breastfeeding.

dos: Do seek lactation support if returning to more breastfeeding is your goal
Do be patient and celebrate small wins at the breast
Do monitor baby's weight and intake regardless of method
donts: Do not force baby's head onto the breast
Do not blame yourself; feeding journeys are complex
Do not abruptly remove all bottles without a safe plan
when_to_expect_improvement: Changes can be slow; some babies gradually accept more breastfeeds over days to weeks with gentle persistence.
red_flags: Baby not gaining weight well on bottles
Parental distress, exhaustion, or conflict around feeding plans
recommended_action_if_red_flags: Reach out to your healthcare provider and a lactation consultant if weight gain or your well-being is affected.
disclaimer: General educational information only.

title_user: My breasts are painfully full at night
peak_timing: Common when baby starts doing longer stretches of sleep or feeding patterns change.
what_it_is: When baby sleeps longer, your breasts may still produce milk on the old schedule and become very full overnight. This can be uncomfortable and may risk blocked ducts.
body_signals: Very full, aching breasts at night or early morning
Difficulty getting comfortable while lying down
baby_signals: Longer night sleep stretches
Strong feeds when finally waking

immediate_relief_steps.title: Offer a feed before you sleep
immediate_relief_steps.description: Feed or dream-feed baby before you go to bed to reduce the length of the longest stretch without milk removal.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Use brief expression for comfort
immediate_relief_steps.description: If you wake very full and uncomfortable, hand-express or pump a small amount just to relieve pressure, not to fully empty.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Support your breasts at night
immediate_relief_steps.description: Wear a soft, supportive sleep bra and use breast pads if leaking bothers you.

dos: Do monitor for lumps or red areas that could indicate a blocked duct
Do adjust how much you express so you don't overstimulate supply
Do enjoy longer sleep stretches when they come
donts: Do not ignore persistent pain, lumps, or fever
Do not regularly fully empty breasts at night if you want supply to settle
Do not wear very tight bras while sleeping
when_to_expect_improvement: Your supply usually adapts to baby's new pattern over days to weeks, and night-time fullness often eases.
red_flags: Hard, painful lump that doesn't improve after feeds
Fever or flu-like symptoms
Red, hot area on the breast
recommended_action_if_red_flags: Seek medical review if you develop signs of blocked duct or mastitis.
disclaimer: General information only; not a substitute for medical advice.
source_notes: Night engorgement with longer baby sleep.

title_user: One of my breasts seems to produce less milk than the other
peak_timing: Often noticed once pumping starts or baby shows a side preference.
what_it_is: It is common for one breast to produce more milk than the other. Mild differences are usually not a problem if baby's overall intake is good.
body_signals: One breast feels fuller or emptier than the other
Different pumping volumes from each side
baby_signals: Preference for one side during feeds
Fussiness on the side with slower flow

immediate_relief_steps.title: Offer the lower-producing side first
immediate_relief_steps.description: Start feeds on the side that produces less so baby's strongest sucking stimulates that breast.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Add extra stimulation if needed
immediate_relief_steps.description: If you pump, consider a few extra minutes on the lower-producing side to signal more demand.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Monitor baby's overall intake
immediate_relief_steps.description: Focus on baby's total wet diapers and weight gain rather than perfect symmetry between breasts.

dos: Do accept some natural left-right differences
Do seek support if one side is consistently painful or very low in output
Do adjust positions to make the less preferred side more comfortable for baby
donts: Do not panic if one side pumps less; many mothers are like this
Do not ignore significant pain or lumps on either side
when_to_expect_improvement: Some differences remain, but small adjustments can improve comfort and output over time.
red_flags: Sudden drop in supply on one side with pain or lumps
Visible deformity or skin changes on one breast
recommended_action_if_red_flags: Seek medical evaluation for sudden or unexplained changes in one breast.
disclaimer: General educational information; not a substitute for a breast exam when needed.

title_user: Someone mentioned my baby might have a tongue tie
peak_timing: Often raised in the first weeks when latch or weight gain is difficult.
what_it_is: Tongue-tie is when the tissue under the tongue is tighter or shorter and may restrict movement. Not all tongue-ties cause problems, but some can affect latch and milk transfer.
body_signals: Nipple pain, damage, or repeated shallow latching
Clicking sounds and poor milk removal
baby_signals: Difficulty staying latched or slipping off
Heart-shaped tongue tip or limited tongue lift
Slow weight gain in some cases

immediate_relief_steps.title: Seek a skilled feeding assessment
immediate_relief_steps.description: Arrange an in-person assessment with a lactation consultant or provider experienced in tongue-tie and breastfeeding.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Optimise latch and positioning
immediate_relief_steps.description: Use techniques that help baby take a deeper mouthful of breast, regardless of tongue appearance.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Monitor weight and symptoms
immediate_relief_steps.description: Keep track of weight gain, diaper counts, and your pain levels while you seek evaluation.

dos: Do ask for a thorough functional assessment, not just a quick look
Do get a second opinion if recommendations are unclear
Do consider your goals and baby's overall health when deciding on treatment
donts: Do not rush into procedures based solely on appearance without a feeding assessment
Do not ignore ongoing severe pain or poor weight gain
when_to_expect_improvement: If treatment is recommended and performed, some families notice changes quickly; for others, improvement takes practice and time.
red_flags: Persistent poor weight gain despite support
Severe maternal nipple trauma that does not improve
Baby shows signs of feeding distress
recommended_action_if_red_flags: Seek coordinated care between your baby's doctor and feeding specialists if problems persist.
disclaimer: This card does not diagnose tongue-tie and cannot replace in-person assessment.

title_user: I have burning nipple pain and shiny skin
peak_timing: Can occur any time, sometimes after antibiotics.
what_it_is: Nipple thrush is a yeast infection on the nipple and areola that can cause burning pain and shiny skin. Baby may also have thrush in the mouth or diaper area.
body_signals: Burning or shooting nipple pain during and after feeds
Shiny or flaky skin on the nipple/areola
Pain in both breasts that lingers between feeds
baby_signals: White patches inside the mouth that do not wipe off easily
Diaper rash that is shiny or not improving

immediate_relief_steps.title: Seek medical diagnosis
immediate_relief_steps.description: Contact your healthcare provider to confirm whether this is thrush and to discuss treatment options for you and baby.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Maintain good hygiene
immediate_relief_steps.description: Change breast pads frequently, wash hands often, and keep nipples dry between feeds.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Manage discomfort
immediate_relief_steps.description: Ask your provider about safe pain relief options and continue breastfeeding if advised.

dos: Do treat both you and baby if thrush is diagnosed
Do wash items like pacifiers, pump parts, and bras thoroughly
Do complete the full course of prescribed treatment
donts: Do not self-diagnose persistent pain without medical input
Do not stop treatment early if symptoms improve
when_to_expect_improvement: With appropriate treatment, many mothers notice improvement in several days, though full resolution may take longer.
red_flags: Worsening pain despite treatment
New symptoms such as fever or spreading redness
recommended_action_if_red_flags: If symptoms persist or worsen, follow up with your provider.
disclaimer: This is general information and does not replace medical diagnosis or treatment.

title_user: My baby is very gassy and fussy after feeds
peak_timing: Common in the first months as digestion matures.
what_it_is: Gas and fussiness after feeds are common as baby's gut develops. Feeding positions, latch, and swallowing air can all contribute.
body_signals: No major breast symptoms; your main concern is baby's behaviour
baby_signals: Pulling legs up, grunting, or passing gas
Crying or fussiness after feeds, especially in the evening
May be harder to settle flat on their back

immediate_relief_steps.title: Review latch and flow
immediate_relief_steps.description: Work toward a deep latch and comfortable flow so baby swallows less air.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Use burping and upright time
immediate_relief_steps.description: Burp baby during and after feeds and hold them upright for a while after feeding.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Offer soothing strategies
immediate_relief_steps.description: Try gentle rocking, white noise, or baby-wearing if baby is otherwise well.

dos: Do track patterns of fussiness and gas
Do talk to your provider if fussiness is severe or you are worried
Do watch for other signs of illness or allergy
donts: Do not shake the baby as a soothing method
Do not assume all crying is due to gas; consider other needs too
when_to_expect_improvement: Many babies become less gassy and fussy as their digestive system matures over the first few months.
red_flags: Vomiting green bile or blood
Distended, hard belly with inconsolable crying
Poor weight gain or blood in stools
recommended_action_if_red_flags: Seek urgent medical care if you see these concerning signs.
disclaimer: General soothing information only; does not replace medical advice.

title_user: I feel pressured to stop breastfeeding before I'm ready
peak_timing: Any time others question or criticise your feeding choices.
what_it_is: Family, friends, or workplace demands can create pressure to wean earlier than you want. Your feeding choices are personal and deserve support.
body_signals: Possible engorgement or discomfort if feeds are skipped suddenly
Fatigue or emotional strain
baby_signals: No specific physical signs; may react to changes in routine

immediate_relief_steps.title: Clarify your own wishes
immediate_relief_steps.description: Reflect on what you want for feeding right now and in the near future, separate from others' opinions.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Seek supportive voices
immediate_relief_steps.description: Talk with people who support your goals, including healthcare providers and peer support groups.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Adjust gradually if you decide to wean
immediate_relief_steps.description: If you choose to wean, do so slowly to protect your comfort and your baby's adjustment.

dos: Do remember that your feeding choices are valid
Do ask about your legal rights and workplace accommodations if relevant
Do take care of your mental health
donts: Do not make big changes overnight due to pressure if you are unsure
Do not ignore your own emotional needs
when_to_expect_improvement: Stress may ease as you find support and make decisions that align with your values.
red_flags: Persistent low mood, hopelessness, or anxiety
Feeling forced into decisions you do not want
recommended_action_if_red_flags: Seek mental health and medical support if you feel overwhelmed, hopeless, or pressured beyond your control.
disclaimer: Emotional support information only; not a substitute for mental health care.

title_user: Breastfeeding makes me feel overwhelmed and tearful
peak_timing: Common in the early weeks when sleep is low and expectations are high.
what_it_is: Feeding around the clock, pain, and pressure can make breastfeeding feel emotionally heavy. This can overlap with baby blues or postpartum mood changes.
body_signals: Exhaustion, tension, or physical pain during feeds
Headaches or body aches from stress
baby_signals: Baby may sense your stress and become fussy, or may feed as usual

immediate_relief_steps.title: Name what you are feeling
immediate_relief_steps.description: It can help to say out loud or write down that breastfeeding feels hard right now and that these feelings are valid.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Ask for practical help
immediate_relief_steps.description: Have someone else handle tasks like meals, laundry, or burping so you can focus on feeding and rest.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Build small breaks into your day
immediate_relief_steps.description: Even a short walk, shower, or quiet cup of tea while someone holds the baby can help recharge you.

dos: Do talk honestly with a trusted person or professional about how you feel
Do screen for postpartum depression or anxiety with your provider
Do consider adjusting your feeding plan if needed for your mental health
donts: Do not suffer in silence
Do not believe you are failing; many mothers feel this way
Do not ignore thoughts of harming yourself or others
when_to_expect_improvement: With support, validation, and rest, many mothers find emotional load lightens over weeks, but persistent symptoms need professional help.
red_flags: Feeling hopeless most of the day for more than two weeks
Loss of interest in things you usually enjoy
Thoughts of harming yourself or your baby
recommended_action_if_red_flags: Seek immediate help from a mental health professional, your doctor, or emergency services if you have thoughts of harming yourself or your baby.
disclaimer: This is not a mental health diagnosis and cannot replace professional care.

title_user: I can't see how much baby is getting and it makes me anxious
peak_timing: Very common in the first weeks when breastfeeding is new.
what_it_is: Unlike bottles, breasts do not show measurements. Many mothers feel anxious about whether baby is getting enough, especially at night or during growth spurts.
body_signals: Tension or tightness during feeds
Racing thoughts or worry focused on milk amount
baby_signals: Feeding patterns may be normal, but you still feel unsure
Cluster feeding or fussiness can increase worry

immediate_relief_steps.title: Use objective signs
immediate_relief_steps.description: Track wet and dirty diapers and attend weight checks to get concrete information on intake.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Limit late-night searching
immediate_relief_steps.description: Set boundaries around scrolling through conflicting advice and focus on trusted sources.

immediate_relief_steps.title: Share the mental load
immediate_relief_steps.description: Talk with your partner or support person so you are not holding all the worry alone.

dos: Do ask your healthcare provider what weight gain and diapers to expect
Do seek reassurance and education from a lactation consultant
Do practice self-kindness; worry often comes from caring deeply
donts: Do not rely solely on apps or social media for reassurance
Do not compare your baby to every other baby's schedule or size
when_to_expect_improvement: Confidence often grows as you see your baby thrive and get used to their feeding patterns over time.
red_flags: Persistent, intrusive worries that interfere with sleep or daily life
Physical symptoms of anxiety like palpitations, shaking, or panic
recommended_action_if_red_flags: If worry feels out of control, speak with your healthcare provider or a mental health professional.
disclaimer: General emotional support information only; not a mental health diagnosis.

title_user: I keep crying for no reason — is something wrong with me?
timing: Peaks around day 3–5 when hormones drop sharply after birth. Typically resolves on its own by day 10–14.
what_it_is: Baby blues are a normal hormonal response to the sudden drop in oestrogen and progesterone after delivery. You may feel weepy, overwhelmed, or emotionally fragile even when things are going well. This affects up to 80% of new mothers and is not a sign of weakness or failure.
body_signals: Bursting into tears unexpectedly
Feeling irritable or emotionally raw
Swinging between happiness and sadness quickly
Difficulty sleeping even when baby sleeps

steps.title: Let yourself cry without judgement
steps.desc: Tears are your body processing a massive hormonal shift and life change. You don't need a reason. Let it out, then rest.

steps.title: Accept help and rest
steps.desc: Ask your partner, mother, or anyone nearby to hold the baby while you sleep, eat, or simply sit quietly. Rest is medicine right now.

steps.title: Stay connected
steps.desc: Talk to someone you trust about how you feel. Even a short conversation reduces the sense of isolation that can intensify the blues.

dos: Do rest as much as possible — sleep deprivation worsens every emotion
Do eat warm nourishing meals; your body is recovering from a huge physical event
Do tell your partner or family how you feel so they can support you
donts: Don't dismiss your feelings or tell yourself to just be happy
Don't be alone more than necessary — isolation intensifies the blues
Don't make major decisions while you're in the thick of it
red_flags: Feelings do not improve at all after 2 weeks
You feel no connection or warmth toward your baby
Thoughts of harming yourself or the baby
when_to_expect: Baby blues almost always lift naturally by day 10–14 as hormones stabilise. If not, speak to your doctor — it may be postpartum depression.
disclaimer: This information is for general education only and is not a substitute for professional medical care. If you are worried, seek help from a qualified health professional.

title_user: I feel empty, hopeless, and disconnected from my baby
timing: Can begin any time in the first year, most commonly within the first 4–6 weeks after birth.
what_it_is: Postpartum depression is a medical condition, not a character flaw or weakness. It involves persistent low mood, difficulty bonding, loss of interest in things you normally enjoy, and sometimes frightening thoughts. It is very treatable and you deserve help.
body_signals: Persistent sadness, emptiness, or numbness lasting more than 2 weeks
Unable to sleep even when baby sleeps, or sleeping too much
Loss of appetite or overeating
Feeling like a bad mother or that your baby would be better off without you

steps.title: Tell someone today
steps.desc: Tell your partner, a family member, or your doctor how you truly feel. PPD does not get better with silence. You don't need to manage this alone.

steps.title: Contact your doctor or midwife
steps.desc: PPD responds well to support, therapy, and sometimes medication. Your doctor can assess you and guide the right treatment. This is a medical appointment, not a sign of failure.

steps.title: Remove pressure temporarily
steps.desc: Hand over as many tasks as possible to others. Your only job right now is to get support. Housework, cooking, and everything else can wait.

dos: Do seek medical care promptly — PPD is highly treatable
Do accept that needing help is not weakness; it is how healing starts
Do let trusted people stay close to you and your baby while you recover
donts: Don't wait hoping it will pass on its own past 2 weeks
Don't feel ashamed — PPD is a medical condition caused by biology, not your choices
Don't be alone with intrusive or frightening thoughts; call someone immediately
red_flags: Thoughts of harming yourself or your baby — call emergency services immediately
Feeling completely detached from reality or hearing/seeing things
Unable to care for yourself or baby at all
when_to_expect: With appropriate treatment, most women with PPD see significant improvement within 3–6 weeks. Recovery is real and full recovery is common.
disclaimer: This information is for general education only and is not a substitute for professional medical care. If you are worried, seek help from a qualified health professional.

title_user: I have strong crampy pains in my belly after birth
timing: Most intense in the first 2–4 days after birth. Often stronger during breastfeeding feeds because oxytocin triggers uterine contractions.
what_it_is: Afterpains are your uterus contracting back to its pre-pregnancy size — a normal and necessary process. They feel like period cramps and are often stronger during feeds. They are more noticeable with second and subsequent babies.
body_signals: Wave-like cramping in the lower abdomen
Pain that intensifies during or just after breastfeeding
Bleeding that increases slightly during cramping episodes

steps.title: Apply a warm compress
steps.desc: A warm wheat bag or hot water bottle (not too hot) placed on your lower belly during a cramping episode brings real relief. Try this especially during feeds.

steps.title: Adjust your position
steps.desc: Lying on your stomach with a pillow under your belly can help. Side-lying during feeds reduces pain for some mothers.

steps.title: Take prescribed pain relief
steps.desc: If your doctor or midwife has recommended paracetamol or ibuprofen, take it about 30 minutes before a feed to stay ahead of the pain.

dos: Do empty your bladder frequently — a full bladder makes cramping worse
Do continue breastfeeding even though it triggers cramps — the contractions are beneficial
Do rest between feeds as much as possible
donts: Don't panic — strong afterpains are normal, especially in the first 2–3 days
Don't stop breastfeeding to avoid the pain; the cramps reduce supply only for a moment
Don't ignore pain that is one-sided, constant, or accompanied by fever
red_flags: Severe one-sided pain that doesn't ease
Fever above 38 °C with abdominal pain
Unusually heavy bleeding soaking more than one pad per hour
when_to_expect: Afterpains usually ease noticeably by day 4–5 and are mostly gone by day 7–10.
disclaimer: This information is for general education only and is not a substitute for professional medical care. If you are worried, seek help from a qualified health professional.

title_user: I have stitches or soreness 'down below' and it hurts to sit
timing: Begins immediately after a vaginal birth. Stitches usually dissolve within 2–4 weeks; full comfort typically returns by 6 weeks.
what_it_is: The perineum (the area between the vagina and the back passage) stretches significantly during a vaginal birth and may have a small tear or cut (episiotomy) that is stitched. Soreness and bruising in this area is completely normal and heals well with good care.
body_signals: Throbbing, stinging, or aching pain between the legs
Discomfort or stinging when passing urine
Difficulty sitting comfortably for the first 1–2 weeks

steps.title: Use a sitz bath or warm water rinse
steps.desc: Soaking in a shallow warm bath for 10–15 minutes several times a day soothes swelling and promotes healing. Pour warm water over the area when urinating to ease the sting.

steps.title: Apply cold for the first 48 hours
steps.desc: Wrap ice or a cold pack in a cloth and apply for 10–15 minutes every few hours. This reduces swelling significantly in the early days.

steps.title: Keep the area clean and dry
steps.desc: Pat gently dry after washing, change maternity pads regularly, and wear breathable cotton underwear. Avoid sitting on hard surfaces — use a soft ring cushion if needed.

dos: Do drink plenty of water and eat fibre to keep stools soft and reduce straining
Do do gentle pelvic floor contractions once the acute pain eases — this improves blood flow and healing
Do check your stitches with a mirror if you are worried; slight pink discharge is normal
donts: Don't use scented soaps, wipes, or bubble bath in the area
Don't strain during bowel movements — use a stool softener if prescribed
Don't return to exercise until cleared by your doctor or physiotherapist
red_flags: Swelling that is getting larger or feels very hot and tight
Foul-smelling or coloured discharge from the wound
Fever with increased pain — may indicate infection
when_to_expect: Significant improvement within 1–2 weeks. Most women are comfortable within 4–6 weeks, though full healing takes up to 3 months.
disclaimer: This information is for general education only and is not a substitute for professional medical care. If you are worried, seek help from a qualified health professional.

title_user: I'm losing alarming amounts of hair — am I going bald?
timing: Typically starts 2–4 months after birth and peaks around month 3–4. Can feel alarming but is almost always temporary.
what_it_is: During pregnancy, high oestrogen keeps hair in the growth phase, so you lose very little. After birth, oestrogen drops and all that extra hair sheds at once. You are not going bald — you are returning to your normal hair density. This affects most new mothers.
body_signals: Large amounts of hair coming out in the shower or brush
Thinning around the temples or a wider parting
Hair on pillows, in the sink, and throughout the house

steps.title: Be gentle with your hair
steps.desc: Use a wide-tooth comb, avoid tight hairstyles, and let hair air-dry when possible. Reduce heat styling and harsh chemical treatments temporarily.

steps.title: Keep nutrition strong
steps.desc: Eat iron-rich foods (daal, green leafy vegetables, nuts, meat), protein, and biotin-rich foods. If you are vegetarian or breastfeeding, ask your doctor about supplements.

steps.title: Accept it and give it time
steps.desc: Knowing why this happens helps reduce the panic. Most hair is fully restored by 12–18 months after birth. Try a new shorter style if the change bothers you visually.

dos: Do continue taking your postnatal vitamins, especially if breastfeeding
Do eat enough protein and iron — deficiency worsens hair loss
Do use a volumising shampoo if hair feels thin
donts: Don't crash diet or restrict calories — this dramatically worsens hair loss
Don't pull hair into very tight ponytails, which cause further breakage
Don't panic — this is not permanent for the vast majority of women
red_flags: Hair loss continues beyond 12 months with no sign of regrowth
Loss of eyebrows or eyelashes as well as scalp hair
Extreme fatigue, cold intolerance, or unexplained weight changes alongside hair loss (may indicate thyroid issue)
when_to_expect: Shedding usually peaks around 3–4 months and slows noticeably by 6 months. Full density typically restored by 12–18 months.
disclaimer: This information is for general education only and is not a substitute for professional medical care. If you are worried, seek help from a qualified health professional.

title_user: I haven't had a proper bowel movement since giving birth
timing: Very common in the first 3–5 days after birth; fear of pain near stitches often delays the first bowel movement.
what_it_is: After birth, pain medications, iron supplements, dehydration, and fear of straining near stitches can all slow down your bowels. The first post-birth bowel movement can feel scary but is rarely as painful as you fear. Stitches will not tear.
body_signals: No bowel movement for 3 or more days after birth
Hard, dry stools that are difficult or painful to pass
Bloating or discomfort in the lower abdomen

steps.title: Drink warm water consistently
steps.desc: Aim for 8–10 glasses of fluid daily. Warm water with a little lemon or jeera (cumin) water in the morning stimulates bowel movement naturally.

steps.title: Eat fibre-rich soft foods
steps.desc: Include papaya, bananas, soaked dried prunes or figs, warm daal, sabzi with ghee, and whole grains. Avoid dry, fried foods that worsen constipation.

steps.title: Move gently and don't delay
steps.desc: Short slow walks encourage gut movement. When you feel the urge, don't hold on — delaying makes stools harder. Use a small footstool under your feet on the toilet to take pressure off the area.

dos: Do take a stool softener if your doctor has prescribed one — this is not a laxative and is safe
Do eat a spoonful of ghee with meals — a traditional practice that genuinely lubricates the gut
Do keep warm fluids going throughout the day
donts: Don't strain hard — hold a pad against your perineum if you feel you need support
Don't eat constipating foods like bananas (ripe ones are fine), white bread, or too much rice immediately after birth
Don't ignore it beyond day 4–5 without speaking to your midwife or doctor
red_flags: No bowel movement for more than 5 days with significant pain
Blood in the stool (small amounts from haemorrhoids is common, but any bright red blood warrants a check)
Severe abdominal pain or vomiting
when_to_expect: Most women have their first bowel movement within 3–5 days of birth. With good hydration and diet, regularity returns within 1–2 weeks.
disclaimer: This information is for general education only and is not a substitute for professional medical care. If you are worried, seek help from a qualified health professional.

title_user: My baby's skin and eyes look yellow
timing: Physiological jaundice typically appears on day 2–3, peaks around day 4–5, and clears by day 10–14 in most term babies. Jaundice in the first 24 hours is always urgent.
what_it_is: Jaundice causes a yellow tinge to the skin and whites of the eyes because the baby's immature liver is processing a substance called bilirubin. Mild jaundice is very common and usually harmless. Severe or rapidly rising jaundice needs prompt treatment.
body_signals: Yellow colour starting on the face and spreading to chest, belly, and limbs
Yellow tinge in the whites of the eyes
Baby is sleepy and difficult to feed

steps.title: Feed frequently — this is the most important step
steps.desc: Feeding 8–12 times a day helps the baby pass bilirubin through bowel movements. If breastfeeding, ensure baby is latching well and transferring milk effectively.

steps.title: Monitor the yellow colour
steps.desc: In natural daylight (not artificial light), press gently on baby's nose or forehead with a finger. If the skin looks yellow when released, check with your doctor. The colour spreading below the knees or into the eyes requires same-day medical review.

steps.title: Attend all follow-up checks
steps.desc: Accept all home visits or clinic checks arranged for your baby in the first week. Bilirubin levels can be measured quickly with a skin device and don't always require a blood test.

dos: Do feed very frequently — this is the single most effective treatment for mild jaundice
Do look at your baby in natural daylight to assess the colour accurately
Do take your baby for a bilirubin check if you are unsure — it is quick and non-invasive
donts: Don't skip feeds or let baby sleep more than 3 hours without waking to feed
Don't use home sunlight exposure as treatment without medical guidance — it is not a substitute for phototherapy
Don't wait to seek help if yellow colour is spreading below the waist or your baby seems very unwell
red_flags: Jaundice appearing in the first 24 hours after birth — always urgent
Yellow colour reaching below the knees or into the palms and soles
Baby is extremely sleepy, not waking to feed, or has a very high-pitched cry
when_to_expect: Mild physiological jaundice clears by 10–14 days in term babies. With phototherapy if needed, levels drop within 24–48 hours of treatment.
disclaimer: This information is for general education only and is not a substitute for professional medical care. If you are worried, seek help from a qualified health professional.

title_user: My baby's bottom is red and sore — diaper rash
timing: Can occur any time but is most common from 4 weeks onwards when stools change. Often flares during teething or after introduction of solid foods.
what_it_is: Diaper rash is redness and inflammation of the skin in the nappy area caused by prolonged contact with urine or stool, friction, or a reaction to wipes or products. It is very common and clears quickly with the right care.
body_signals: Red, inflamed skin in the nappy area
Baby cries or seems uncomfortable during nappy changes
Skin may look raw, bumpy, or have small spots

steps.title: Change nappies frequently
steps.desc: Check and change the nappy every 2–3 hours and immediately after every bowel movement. Leaving a wet or soiled nappy on even briefly allows the rash to worsen.

steps.title: Clean gently and let air-dry
steps.desc: Use plain warm water and a soft cloth (or fragrance-free wipes). Pat dry gently — never rub. Allow a few minutes of nappy-free time with the area fully open to air whenever practical.

steps.title: Apply a thick barrier cream
steps.desc: Use a zinc oxide-based barrier cream (like Drapolene, Sudocrem, or plain zinc paste available in any pharmacy) at every change. Apply a thick layer — don't rub it in.

dos: Do let baby have nappy-free time on a towel in a warm room each day
Do use fragrance-free, alcohol-free products near the nappy area
Do apply fresh barrier cream at every single nappy change until fully healed
donts: Don't use talcum powder — it can be inhaled and doesn't help the rash
Don't use scented wipes or soaps in the nappy area
Don't put a tight nappy on — allow some air flow
red_flags: Rash spreading beyond the nappy area onto the abdomen or thighs
Bright red beefy rash with satellite spots at the edges (suggests fungal infection)
Blisters, open sores, or pus-filled spots
when_to_expect: With frequent changes and barrier cream, most diaper rash clears within 3–4 days. Fungal rashes need antifungal cream and take about a week.
disclaimer: This information is for general education only and is not a substitute for professional medical care. If you are worried, seek help from a qualified health professional.

title_user: My baby cries inconsolably every evening for hours
timing: Typically begins around week 2–3, peaks around week 6, and resolves on its own by 3–4 months in most babies.
what_it_is: Colic is defined as crying for more than 3 hours a day, more than 3 days a week, in an otherwise healthy baby under 3 months. The cause is not fully understood but is real and exhausting. It is not caused by bad parenting and it does end.
body_signals: Intense, high-pitched crying that is difficult to console
Baby draws up legs, clenches fists, and arches back
Crying episodes tend to occur at the same time each day, often evening
Baby may pass wind or appear to be in pain

steps.title: Try movement and gentle pressure
steps.desc: Hold baby face-down along your forearm (the 'colic hold'), rock gently, walk, or use a baby carrier. The combination of warmth, pressure on the belly, and movement soothes many babies.

steps.title: Use white noise or shushing
steps.desc: A strong 'shhhh' sound at the level of baby's cries, a running tap, or a white noise app can help. The womb was very loud — familiar sound is comforting.

steps.title: Tag-team with your partner
steps.desc: Set a time limit — if baby is crying and you have tried everything, hand over to your partner after 20 minutes and take a short break. Parental distress is heard and felt by the baby.

dos: Do ensure baby is winded thoroughly after every feed — sit baby upright and pat the back gently for 10–15 minutes
Do try Infacol or gripe water (available over the counter) if recommended by your pharmacist
Do accept that sometimes nothing works — holding through it is enough
donts: Don't shake or roughly handle the baby out of frustration — put baby down safely and step outside for a moment if you need to
Don't change formula repeatedly without medical advice — this rarely helps
Don't blame yourself — colic is not caused by anything you are doing wrong
red_flags: Baby has a fever above 38 °C alongside the crying
Baby is not feeding or is vomiting forcefully
The crying pattern changes suddenly or baby seems unwell between episodes
when_to_expect: Colic typically resolves completely by 3–4 months of age. Each week before then may still be hard, but there is a clear endpoint.
disclaimer: This information is for general education only and is not a substitute for professional medical care. If you are worried, seek help from a qualified health professional.

title_user: My baby has lost weight since birth — is that normal?
timing: Expected weight loss occurs in the first 3–5 days of life and birth weight is normally regained by day 10–14.
what_it_is: Almost all newborns lose weight in the first few days as they pass meconium, lose excess fluid, and adjust to feeding. A loss of up to 7% of birth weight is considered normal. Beyond 10% is a signal to investigate feeding and possibly supplement. It does not mean you have failed.
body_signals: Baby may seem sleepy, with fewer wet or dirty nappies than expected
Baby may appear slightly yellow-tinged (often linked)
You may notice less milk on the breast pad or fewer swallows during feeds

steps.title: Feed at least 8–12 times in 24 hours
steps.desc: Wake your baby for feeds if they sleep longer than 3 hours. Frequency of feeding is the single most important driver of weight gain in the first two weeks.

steps.title: Confirm effective milk transfer
steps.desc: Look for a rhythmic suck-swallow-breathe pattern during feeds, hear quiet swallowing, and see wet and dirty nappies. If unsure, ask for a weighted feed assessment with a lactation consultant.

steps.title: Attend every weight check
steps.desc: Accept all weight checks offered in the first two weeks. Tracking the trend is more important than any single number.

dos: Do look for output as a sign of input — wet nappies and stools tell you baby is getting milk
Do seek a lactation consultant review if weight loss exceeds 7% or regain is slow
Do keep baby close and feed on demand throughout the day and night
donts: Don't be afraid to ask for a weight check if you are worried — this is always appropriate
Don't offer formula without medical guidance as the first response to weight loss
Don't wait more than 24 hours if baby is losing weight rapidly or seems unwell
red_flags: Weight loss exceeding 10% of birth weight
Birth weight not regained by day 14
Baby showing signs of dehydration: no wet nappy in 12 hours, sunken fontanelle, dry mouth
when_to_expect: With good feeding, weight should stabilise by day 4–5 and return to birth weight by day 10–14. Most babies gain 150–200 g per week after that.
disclaimer: This information is for general education only and is not a substitute for professional medical care. If you are worried, seek help from a qualified health professional.
